panels.description: Logarithmic scale
panels.title: RPS by Method (log scale)

panels.description: Logarithmic scale
panels.title: p99 Latency by Method (log scale)

panels.description: Client-side gRPC error codes (NotFound, InvalidArgument, AlreadyExists, PermissionDenied, OutOfRange, Unimplemented, Unauthenticated, FailedPrecondition, Canceled).
panels.title: 4xx Errors (log scale)

panels.description: Server-side gRPC error codes (Unknown, Unavailable, DeadlineExceeded, ResourceExhausted, Aborted, Internal).
panels.title: 5xx Errors (log scale)

panels.description: p99 latency of API calls broken down by permission shape (resource type/relation, subject type/relation). Identifies which specific permission checks are slowest.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(spicedb_perf_insights_api_shape_latency_seconds_bucket{job="spicedb",instance=~"$instance",resource_type!=""}[$__rate_interval])) by (le, api_kind, resource_type, resource_relation, subject_type, subject_relation))

panels.title: p99 Latency by API Shape (log scale) - needs perf insights

panels.description: Average logical checks per second across all APIs. Counts each item within a CheckBulk and each result returned from LookupResources/LookupSubjects separately. This measures workload intensity independently of gRPC RPS — a single LookupResources call can account for hundreds of logical checks.
panels.title: Avg Logical Checks per Second

panels.description: Rate of requests per consistency mode (minimize_latency, at_least_as_fresh, fully_consistent, at_exact_snapshot). A high share of fully_consistent requests increases database load; minimize_latency requests can be served from cache.
panels.title: Avg Consistency Mode Distribution

panels.description: Distribution of request rate across instances. If one instance receives significantly more traffic than others, there may be a load balancing issue in front of the instances.
panels.title: RPS Distribution by Instance

panels.description: Distribution of dispatch request rate across instances. If one instance receives significantly more dispatch traffic than others, it may suggest a problem with dispatching or recursive permission check distribution.
panels.title: Dispatch RPS Distribution by Instance

panels.description: Cache hit ratio for each dispatch operation type, split by dispatcher layer. High ratios (>80%) for check operations are expected under steady workloads. Low ratios indicate cache churn, highly diverse queries, or an undersized cache. Per-instance variance is expected and normal: the dispatch cache is local in-process (not shared across instances), and every cache key is scoped to a specific revision — so any write that advances the revision causes all instances to miss on subsequent requests until they re-warm at the new revision. Instances that recently restarted or received the first requests after a revision bump will show lower ratios than instances with a warm cache at a stable revision.
panels.title: Dispatch Cache Hit Ratio by Operation Type

panels.description: p99 of the number of chunks dispatched per Check request. Each chunk holds up to dispatch-chunk-size subjects (default: 100) and is processed in parallel. A value of N means the check fanned out to N×chunk-size subjects of the same type. Consistently high values indicate broad permission checks with large subject fan-out.
panels.title: p99 Check Dispatch Chunk Count

panels.description:
Hit ratio per cache, from spicedb_cache_hits_total and spicedb_cache_misses_total. Three caches emit metrics:

• namespace (32MiB): caches parsed namespace and caveat definitions. Should be near 100% — schemas change infrequently. A sustained drop means schema writes are frequent or the cache is undersized.

• dispatch (default: 30% of free memory): caches sub-problem results for dispatches this node sends out (outbound). Revision-scoped — every write that advances the revision invalidates prior entries.

• cluster_dispatch (default: 70% of free memory): caches sub-problem results for dispatches this node receives from other nodes (inbound). Also revision-scoped. Larger by default because in a multi-node cluster this node handles a proportionally larger share of inbound sub-problems.
panels.title: Cache Hit Ratio - $instance

panels.description: Logarithmic scale
panels.title: RPS by Datastore Operation

panels.description: Logarithmic scale
panels.title: p99 Latency by Datastore Operation

panels.description: p99 of the number of relationships loaded per datastore query. High values may indicate inefficient queries or broad permission checks that load many relationships.
panels.title: p99 Relationships Loaded per Query

panels.description: p99 of the number of relationship updates per WriteRelationships call, split by update kind (CREATE, TOUCH, DELETE). Large batches (>1000) can cause write amplification and lock contention in the datastore.
panels.title: p99 WriteRelationships Batch Size - $instance

panels.description: Current number of healthy CockroachDB nodes as detected by SpiceDB. A value below the expected cluster size indicates a node is down or unreachable. Any value of 0 is critical.
panels.title: Healthy CRDB Nodes

panels.description: Number of active connections SpiceDB holds to each CockroachDB node, split by pool (read/write). Significant imbalance across node_id values indicates the connection balancer is not redistributing connections evenly.
panels.title: Connections per CRDB Node

panels.description: Rate of client-side transaction restarts per second (left axis) and p99 number of restarts per transaction attempt (right axis). Restarts occur when CockroachDB returns a serialization failure (40001) and the client retries from scratch. A rising rate indicates transaction contention on the cluster.
panels.title: CRDB Client-side Transaction Reset Rate

panels.description: p99 and p50 duration in milliseconds of one iteration of the CockroachDB connection balancer pruning excess connections from over-represented nodes. Elevated values (>10ms) indicate the balancer is struggling to redistribute connections.
panels.title: CRDB Connection Pruning Duration (p50/p99)

panels.description: Shows the distribution of connections in the pool. High acquired conns may indicate active load. Low idle conns suggest the pool is under pressure and may need tuning. Constructing conns indicate new connections being established.
panels.title: Connection Pool State - Read ($instance)

panels.description: Shows the distribution of connections in the pool. High acquired conns may indicate active load. Low idle conns suggest the pool is under pressure and may need tuning. Constructing conns indicate new connections being established.
panels.title: Connection Pool State - Write ($instance)

panels.description: Tracks connection acquisition patterns. High acquire count with low canceled/empty indicates healthy pool usage. High canceled acquires may indicate timeout issues or request cancellation. High empty acquires suggest the pool is frequently exhausted and requests are waiting.
panels.title: Connection Acquisition Rate - Read ($instance)

panels.description: Tracks connection acquisition patterns. High acquire count with low canceled/empty indicates healthy pool usage. High canceled acquires may indicate timeout issues or request cancellation. High empty acquires suggest the pool is frequently exhausted and requests are waiting.
panels.title: Connection Acquisition Rate - Write ($instance)

panels.description: Monitors connection creation and destruction. High new conn count indicates connections are being created. High destroy counts suggest connections are being cycled frequently, which may indicate configuration issues with MaxConnIdleTime or MaxConnLifetime.
panels.title: Connection Lifecycle - Read ($instance)

panels.description: Monitors connection creation and destruction. High new conn count indicates connections are being created. High destroy counts suggest connections are being cycled frequently, which may indicate configuration issues with MaxConnIdleTime or MaxConnLifetime.
panels.title: Connection Lifecycle - Write ($instance)

panels.description: Average time spent waiting to acquire a connection from the read pool (nanoseconds). Elevated values mean requests are blocking on pool exhaustion. Consider increasing MaxConns or investigating connection leaks if this rises consistently.
panels.title: Average Connection Acquisition Time - Read ($instance)

panels.description: Average time spent waiting to acquire a connection from the write pool (nanoseconds). Elevated values mean requests are blocking on pool exhaustion. Consider increasing MaxConns or investigating connection leaks if this rises consistently.
panels.title: Average Connection Acquisition Time - Write ($instance)

panels.description: Current state of the sql.DB connection pool: open connections (in-use + idle), in-use connections, idle connections, and the configured maximum. A pool consistently at max_open_connections may indicate saturation.
panels.title: Connection Pool State ($instance)

panels.description: Rates of connection wait events and connection closures. High wait_count indicates goroutines are blocked acquiring connections. Closures due to max_idle, max_idle_time, or max_lifetime suggest aggressive pool recycling.
panels.title: Connection Wait & Closures ($instance)

panels.description: Ratios (0–1) reflecting Spanner session pool health. max_allowed_sessions_ratio approaching 1 means the pool is near its configured limit. max_in_use_sessions_ratio shows peak concurrent session usage. num_sessions_in_pool_ratio and open_session_count_ratio show current utilization.
panels.title: Session Pool Ratios ($instance)

panels.description: Rate of Spanner sessions acquired and released from the pool, plus the rate of GFE (Google Front End) responses missing the server-timing header — a non-zero gfe_header_missing_count may indicate requests bypassing the GFE or connectivity issues.
panels.title: Session Acquisition & GFE Errors ($instance)

panels.description: Rate of datastore GC run failures per second. Any non-zero value means the background garbage collection job is failing. Sustained failures cause unbounded table growth in CRDB/PostgreSQL/MySQL and will eventually impact query performance.
panels.title: GC Failure Rate - $instance

panels.description: p99 duration of a single datastore garbage collection run. GC runs compete with normal query traffic. Runs regularly exceeding 10 seconds should be investigated; runs above 60 seconds may be impacting query latency.
panels.title: p99 GC Run Duration - $instance

panels.description: Rate of objects deleted by the background garbage collection job. Stale relationships and transactions are normal artefacts of MVCC-based datastores (CRDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL). Expired relationships are those with an explicit expiry time set at write time. A sudden spike typically follows a large write burst.
panels.title: GC Deleted Items Rate - $instance

panels.description: CPU time consumed by the process, measured in CPU cores. A value of 1.0 means one full core is in use. Compare against GOMAXPROCS to assess CPU saturation.
panels.title: CPU Usage - $instance

panels.description: The number of goroutines reported by the Go runtime.
panels.title: Goroutines - $instance

panels.description: Garbage collection pause duration (p99) and total GC cycle count. High pause durations or frequent GC cycles may indicate memory pressure.
panels.title: GC (garbage collection) - $instance

panels.description: Heap memory in use versus the GOMEMLIMIT cap, and memory usage percentage as reported by the memory middleware. Sustained usage near the limit may trigger throttling.
panels.title: Heap Memory Usage - $instance

panels.description: The number of heap allocations (mallocs) as a rate (malloc/sec).
panels.title: Memory Allocation Rate (malloc/sec) - $instance

panels.description: Shows the number of heap allocations (as a byte/sec rate).
panels.title: Heap Allocation Rate - $instance

panels.description: Time goroutines spend blocked waiting to acquire mutexes (as a rate). Elevated values indicate lock contention.
panels.title: Mutex wait time - $instance

panels.description: Rate of requests rejected by the memory protection middleware. Any rejected requests mean SpiceDB returned ResourceExhausted to callers due to memory pressure.
panels.title: Memory Protection Request Rejection
